era_context: The early 14th century saw the fragmentation of Mongol unity following Kublai Khan's death (1294). The Yuan Dynasty under Ayurbarwada ruled China; the Ilkhanate under Öljeitü dominated Persia; the Golden Horde controlled the Russian steppes. In Europe, the Capetian dynasty ruled France under Louis X; England was under Edward II; Scotland was consolidating independence under Robert the Bruce. This year is marked by the Battle of Bannockburn, a decisive Scottish victory that secured Scottish independence.

events.title: Battle of Bannockburn; Scottish victory and independence secured
events.description: On 23–24 June 1314, Scottish forces under Robert the Bruce defeated a much larger English army led by Edward II at Bannockburn, near Stirling. The Scottish victory was decisive and unexpected; English cavalry charges were repelled by Scottish schiltrons (pike formations), and the English army was routed. Bannockburn effectively ended English military dominance in Scotland and secured Robert the Bruce's position as King of Scotland. The battle had profound consequences: it established Scottish independence (formally recognized in the Declaration of Arbroath, 1320), prevented English conquest of Scotland, and demonstrated the vulnerability of feudal cavalry to disciplined infantry formations.
key_figures: Robert the Bruce (King of Scotland, r. 1306–1329)
Thomas Randolph, Earl of Moray (Scottish commander)
Aymer de Valence, Earl of Pembroke (English commander)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Bannockburn is one of the most well-documented medieval battles. The date (23–24 June 1314), the commanders, and the outcome are secure.
cross_references: 1328-001 (Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton, formal recognition of Scottish independence)

events.title: Death of Pope Clement V; papal succession crisis
events.description: Pope Clement V died on 20 April 1314 in Roquemaure, France. His death triggered a succession crisis and a two-year papal vacancy (sede vacante) that lasted until the election of Pope John XXII in August 1316. Clement V had been the first pope to reside in Avignon (rather than Rome), establishing the 'Avignon Papacy' (1309–1377), which would have profound consequences for papal authority and the Church's relationship with secular powers. Clement V's death also marked the end of his protection of the Knights Templar; his successor would face renewed pressure to suppress the order.
events.certainty_note: Clement V's death on 20 April 1314 is well-documented in papal records. The sede vacante lasted until August 1316.

events.title: Louis X crowned King of France
events.description: Louis X (Louis le Hutin, 'the Quarrelsome') was crowned King of France in 1314, following the death of his father Philip IV (r. 1285–1314). Louis X's reign was troubled from the start; he faced baronial opposition, financial difficulties, and conflicts with the Church. His reign lasted only two years before his death in 1316, but his brief rule set the stage for the succession crisis that would follow and the eventual exclusion of women from the French throne.
events.certainty_note: Louis X's coronation in 1314 is well-documented in royal records and chronicles.

events.title: Death of Philip IV of France
events.description: Philip IV (Philip the Fair), one of the most powerful medieval French kings, died on 29 November 1314. His death marked the end of an era of strong Capetian rule and the beginning of the dynasty's decline. Philip IV had expanded French territory, centralized royal authority, and engaged in conflicts with the Papacy and the Knights Templar. His death triggered a succession crisis; his sons Louis X, Philip V, and Charles IV would rule in succession, but none had sons who survived to adulthood, leading to the extinction of the direct Capetian line and the eventual rise of the Valois dynasty.
events.certainty_note: Philip IV's death on 29 November 1314 is well-documented in royal records and chronicles.

events.title: Ayurbarwada (Emperor Renzong) continues reign in Yuan Dynasty
events.description: Ayurbarwada (Emperor Renzong, r. 1311–1320) continued his reign over the Yuan Dynasty in 1314. His reign was marked by efforts to stabilize the dynasty and promote Confucian learning. In 1314, the Yuan Dynasty was economically prosperous and controlled most of East Asia, though facing long-term challenges from regional instability and the rise of Han Chinese resistance movements. Ayurbarwada's reign represented a period of relative stability after the tumultuous reign of his predecessor, Kulug Khan.

sources.name: Yuanshi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Ayurbarwada's reign dates (1311–1320) are well-documented in the Yuanshi.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Bannockburn's significance for Scottish independence is sometimes overstated in popular accounts. While the battle was a decisive military victory, Scottish independence was not formally recognized until the Declaration of Arbroath (1320) and the Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton (1328). The battle did not immediately end English claims to Scottish overlordship; Edward II and his successors continued to assert claims to Scotland for decades. Additionally, some modern historians argue that the schiltron formation, while effective at Bannockburn, was not as revolutionary as sometimes portrayed; pike formations had been used in medieval warfare before 1314. Philip IV's death in 1314 is sometimes attributed to a curse allegedly placed by the Knights Templar (whom he had suppressed), but this is a legendary embellishment with no historical basis.
historiographic_note: The year 1314 is well-documented for Western Europe, thanks to contemporary chronicles, royal records, and military accounts. The Battle of Bannockburn is one of the most thoroughly documented medieval battles. However, sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; the experiences of common soldiers, peasants, and non-combatants are largely invisible. For Asia, documentation is more sparse in Western scholarship; Chinese sources exist but are less accessible. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are virtually absent from this year's record in English-language sources.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Bannockburn's victory enabled Robert the Bruce to consolidate Scottish independence, formalized in the Declaration of Arbroath (1320).
Clement V's death triggered a papal vacancy that lasted until the election of Pope John XXII in 1316.
Philip IV's death directly led to Louis X's coronation as King of France.
Philip IV's death initiated a succession crisis that would eventually lead to the extinction of the direct Capetian line and the rise of the Valois dynasty.
